key_findings: Three-layer scheduler policy: WFQ with debt tracking (base fairness), Starvation Floor Guard (hard minimum guarantee per lane per 60s), Burst Headroom Controller (DNS consecutive dispatch cap) — each layer addresses a distinct failure mode

WFQ debt-tracking produces weighted scheduling ratios (DNS 50%, HTTP 30%, Enrich 20%) without hard rate limits — utilizes oracle fully during high-throughput DNS phases while guaranteeing proportional access to all lanes
Starvation floor guarantees minimum 1 dispatch per lane per 60-second window for any lane with pending work — starvation incidents = 0 by construction; force-dispatch is the hard safety net below WFQ

Burst headroom cap (dns_max_consecutive_dispatches=3) prevents DNS burst from occupying oracle continuously; forces yield to HTTP/Enrich after 3 consecutive DNS dispatches — prevents executor-time starvation even when WFQ weights are satisfied
Debt accumulation bounded by max_debt_cap=10.0 and debt_reset_idle_sec=60 — prevents lanes from accumulating unbounded debt during idle periods and causing burst imbalance when they become active

Utilization band target 75-90%: below 75% = oracle underutilized (increase batch_size or frequency); above 90% = approaching saturation (reduce burst cap); variance reduction target >=20% vs naive FIFO
starvation_floor_activations ORS signal: if >3/hr per lane, WFQ weights are mistuned for current workload; hot-reloadable weights enable in-operation rebalancing without restart
KRIL ordering preserved within each lane queue — starvation floor force-dispatch selects highest-KRIL item within the lane; fairness applies between lanes, not within lanes

decisions.title: WFQ with debt-tracking over round-robin or strict rate limits
decisions.summary: Weighted debt accumulation selects highest-debt lane at each tick; produces weighted scheduling ratios without hard throughput caps
decisions.rationale: Round-robin ignores lane throughput differences: fast DNS lane gets same slots as slow Enrich, wasting oracle capacity. Strict rate limits artificially idle oracle during DNS-heavy phases. WFQ produces correct utilization ratios while adapting to actual workload mix.

decisions.title: Starvation floor as separate hard guarantee below WFQ
decisions.summary: WFQ debt ratios guarantee fair share over long windows; starvation floor guarantees minimum 1 dispatch per 60s window for any pending lane
decisions.rationale: WFQ over infinite windows produces correct ratios, but in finite windows (60s), a heavily loaded DNS queue can starve Enrich. The floor is the safety net that WFQ theory alone cannot provide in finite time.

decisions.title: Burst headroom cap via max_consecutive_dispatches per lane
decisions.summary: After N consecutive dispatches of the same lane, scheduler forces yield to any other eligible lane regardless of debt ordering
decisions.rationale: WFQ addresses scheduling slot fairness. But a single large-batch dispatch (500 DNS records) occupies oracle executor for many seconds, starving other lanes of execution time even if they have scheduling slots. Consecutive cap enforces executor-time fairness.

decisions.title: Debt cap and idle reset to bound debt accumulation
decisions.summary: max_debt_cap=10.0 prevents unbounded debt during idle; debt_reset_idle_sec=60 resets debt after lane has been idle >60s
decisions.rationale: Without a cap, a lane that is idle for hours accumulates enormous debt. When it becomes active, WFQ dispatches it exclusively until debt is consumed — effectively causing burst starvation of other lanes. Cap and reset bound this effect.

decisions.title: KRIL ordering preserved within each lane queue
decisions.summary: Fairness policy governs between-lane scheduling; within each lane, KRIL-ordered priority queue governs which item is dispatched
decisions.rationale: Fairness between lanes ensures all lane types get service. Value ordering within each lane ensures the most valuable work within each type is processed first. These are orthogonal concerns; combining them would require complex cross-lane ordering.

scheduler_layers.name: WFQ Debt Tracker
scheduler_layers.mechanism: Per-lane debt accumulation at each tick proportional to weight; highest-debt eligible lane selected for dispatch

scheduler_layers.name: Starvation Floor Guard
scheduler_layers.mechanism: At end of each 60s window, any lane with pending work that received 0 dispatches gets a forced dispatch

scheduler_layers.name: Burst Headroom Controller
scheduler_layers.mechanism: Per-lane consecutive dispatch counter; after max_consecutive_dispatches, force yield to other eligible lanes
scheduler_layers.rationale: Prevents executor-time starvation even when WFQ scheduling slots are weighted correctly; large DNS batches otherwise monopolize oracle wall-clock time

starvation_incident_definition.definition: A lane with pending work that receives 0 dispatches in a 60-second window
starvation_incident_definition.guarantee_mechanism: starvation_floor layer forces minimum 1 dispatch per window

variance_reduction.baseline_model: naive FIFO dispatch; DNS dominates during peak; sigma ~18%
variance_reduction.measurement: std_dev of oracle utilization across 1-minute intervals over 72h

risk_model.title: WFQ weights mistuned for actual lane throughput
risk_model.mitigation: starvation_floor_activations ORS signal alerts if >3/hr; hot-reloadable weights enable in-operation rebalancing

risk_model.title: Burst cap too aggressive: DNS underutilizes oracle
risk_model.mitigation: utilization_below_floor ORS reflex; relax dns_max_consecutive_dispatches via hot-reload

risk_model.title: Debt burst on lane reactivation after idle
Starvation floor force-dispatch disrupts KRIL ordering
risk_model.mitigation: Force-dispatch still selects highest-KRIL item within the lane queue; KRIL order preserved within lanes

risk_model.title: Oracle saturation despite burst cap
risk_model.mitigation: utilization_above_ceiling ORS reflex reduces batch_size; alert if sustained >5 min

risk_model.title: Hot-reload disrupts in-flight dispatches
risk_model.mitigation: Hot-reload applies at next tick only; in-flight dispatches complete with previous config

risk_model.title: Scheduler tick overhead at 1s
risk_model.mitigation: Tick is O(n_lanes=3) arithmetic; negligible CPU cost on controller

recommended_actions.action: Implement WFQ debt tracker with weights 0.50/0.30/0.20 (DNS/HTTP/Enrich) as base scheduler — delivers weighted fairness from day 1
Add starvation floor guard (60s window, min 1 dispatch per pending lane) — hard guarantee against starvation; immediately eliminates starvation incidents
Set burst cap: DNS=3, HTTP=5, Enrich=10 consecutive dispatches before yield — prevents executor-time starvation during DNS burst phases
Emit starvation_floor_activations as ORS signal; alert if >3/hr per lane — indicates WFQ weights need retuning for current workload
Monitor oracle utilization every 30s; emit ORS signals for <75% and >90% — drives adaptive batch_size tuning
Set debt_reset_idle_sec=60 and max_debt_cap=10.0 — bounds debt accumulation during idle periods and prevents reactivation burst
Run 72-hour simulation with 70/20/10 DNS/HTTP/Enrich workload mix — validates starvation=0, utilization band, variance reduction before production
Make all scheduler parameters hot-reloadable via scheduler_config.json — enables in-operation weight rebalancing without restart

assumptions: A1: Three lanes (DNS, HTTP, Enrich) are the complete set; new lanes require weight re-normalization
A2: Oracle execution time is measurable at the controller for utilization calculation
A3: Scheduler tick interval of 1s achievable without controller CPU bottleneck
A4: Starvation floor window of 60s is acceptable minimum service guarantee
A5: Lane queue depth readable by scheduler at each tick (in-memory queue on controller)
A6: scheduler_config.json hot-reload does not interrupt in-flight oracle dispatches
